items.prompt: Why might synthetic face images fail for face-recognition training (Example 10.4)?
choices: They always contain too much personal identifiable information
They may lack natural variation such as lighting changes and occlusions
They cannot be generated by any algorithm
They always outperform real images in validation
items.explain: Example 10.4 notes synthetic faces may look similar yet miss variation found in real images.

items.prompt: Synthetic data can inherit biases present in the datasets used to train generators.
items.explain: Section 10.2.2 and Example 10.5 describe bias propagation from source data through GANs and other generators.

items.prompt: Overfitting in synthetic data generation often leads to:
choices: Homogeneous samples that miss edge cases and real-world diversity
items.explain: Section 10.2.3 warns that overfitting can produce overly homogeneous synthetic data with poor real-world coverage.

items.prompt: What validation concern arises when a model trained on synthetic data performs well in testing but poorly in deployment?
items.rubric: Mention generalization gap, failure to capture real-world complexity, or mismatch between synthetic and live conditions.
items.sample_answer: The synthetic data may not capture real-world complexity and variation, so the model generalizes poorly outside the synthetic training environment.

items.prompt: Statistical similarity alone guarantees that synthetic data is ready for production model training.
items.explain: Section 10.2.1 stresses that synthetic data can be statistically similar yet miss important structure, noise, or edge behavior.
